title: Pass FTMO with ICT Strategy
seoTitle: How to Pass FTMO Challenge ICT | R2F Trading Complete Guide
seoDescription: Learn how to pass the FTMO challenge using ICT strategy. Harvest Wright breaks down entry models, risk rules & prop firm tactics that actually work.
headline: How to Pass the FTMO Challenge Using ICT — Without Blowing Your Account
subheadline: Most traders fail prop firm challenges not because they lack skill — but because they trade without a structured ICT framework. This guide gives you the exact blueprint Harvest Wright uses to navigate FTMO rules, manage drawdown, and hit profit targets consistently.

keyPoints.title: What the FTMO Challenge Really Tests
keyPoints.text: The FTMO Challenge isn't just about hitting a 10% profit target — it's a 30-day test of discipline, consistency, and risk management. You must stay within a 5% daily and 10% max drawdown while proving you can trade a repeatable system. Understanding these rules before you enter a single trade is the foundation of passing.

keyPoints.title: Why ICT Strategy Is Built for Prop Firms
keyPoints.text: ICT methodology — built around liquidity, market structure, and institutional order flow — naturally aligns with prop firm risk requirements. Because ICT entries are precision-based with defined invalidation points, position sizing stays tight and drawdown stays controlled. You're not guessing; you're trading with the smart money, which is exactly what FTMO rewards.

keyPoints.title: The ICT Framework for Passing FTMO
keyPoints.text: Start each session by identifying the higher timeframe draw on liquidity — where is price engineered to go? Then drop to the 15-minute or 5-minute chart and wait for a Market Structure Shift (MSS) after a liquidity sweep to confirm your entry. Use Fair Value Gaps (FVGs) or Optimal Trade Entry (OTE) within the killzones — London or New York — to execute with a minimum 1:2 risk-to-reward, keeping daily risk under 1% of account equity.

keyPoints.title: The Mistakes That Blow FTMO Accounts
keyPoints.text: The most common failure is revenge trading after a losing session — chasing losses and violating the 5% daily drawdown rule in a single afternoon. A close second is overtrading outside killzone hours when institutional liquidity is absent, turning high-probability setups into coin flips. Treat every trading day like a business operation: no setup, no trade — no exceptions.

keyPoints.title: Your Next Step: Trade Live With a Proven System
keyPoints.text: Ready to attempt your FTMO Challenge with a real ICT edge? Join R2F Trading and get access to Harvest Wright's live trade breakdowns, challenge-specific risk protocols, and a community of ICT traders actively passing prop firm evaluations. Start with a free strategy session at r2ftrading.com and build your funded account from the ground up.
